questions.q: In the episode of the Burning of Kamadeva, which deity opens his third eye to reduce Kamadeva to ashes?
questions.explanation: In the Puranas, it is Shiva who opens his third eye and burns Kamadeva, the god of love, to ashes.

questions.q: Kamadeva, who was burned by Shiva in this episode, is revered in Hindu tradition as the deity of what?
questions.explanation: Kamadeva is the Hindu deity of love and desire, whose very name 'kama' means desire.

questions.q: Toward which goddess were the gods hoping to rouse Shiva's love when they sent Kamadeva?
questions.explanation: The gods sent Kamadeva to awaken Shiva's love for Parvati, so that a son could be born to them.

questions.q: What weapon does Kamadeva loose at Shiva, an act that provokes his fiery destruction?
questions.explanation: Kamadeva shoots a flower-arrow at Shiva, and it is this act that causes Shiva to open his third eye in anger.

questions.q: After being burned, by what name meaning 'the bodiless one' is the revived Kamadeva known?
questions.explanation: Shiva revived Kamadeva as Ananga, meaning 'the bodiless one,' since his physical form had been destroyed.

questions.q: Who is the grieving wife of Kamadeva that pleads with Shiva to restore her husband?
questions.explanation: Rati, the wife of Kamadeva, pleads with Shiva, who then revives Kamadeva as the bodiless Ananga.

questions.q: In the Burning of Kamadeva, the gods' larger purpose was to ensure the birth of a son who would defeat which demon?
questions.explanation: The gods needed a son of Shiva and Parvati to defeat the demon Taraka, who could only be slain by Shiva's offspring.

questions.q: What was Shiva doing when Kamadeva approached to rouse his desire for Parvati?
questions.explanation: Shiva was absorbed in deep meditation when Kamadeva attempted to disturb him with his flower-arrow.

questions.q: Which distinctive feature of Shiva is the source of the fire that burns Kamadeva to ashes?
questions.explanation: The fire that consumes Kamadeva blazes forth from Shiva's third eye, the eye of insight and destruction.

questions.q: The son of Shiva and Parvati who ultimately slays the demon Taraka, the goal of this whole episode, is which deity?
questions.explanation: Kartikeya, also called Skanda or Murugan, is the son born to Shiva and Parvati who defeats the demon Taraka.

questions.q: The Sanskrit poet Kalidasa retells the Burning of Kamadeva in which celebrated epic poem about Shiva and Parvati?
questions.explanation: Kalidasa's Kumarasambhava ('The Birth of the War-God') narrates the burning of Kamadeva and the union of Shiva and Parvati.

questions.q: In iconography, what is the bow of Kamadeva traditionally described as being made of?
questions.explanation: Kamadeva's bow is famously made of sugarcane, with a bowstring formed of a line of humming bees.

questions.q: Kamadeva's arrows are traditionally said to be tipped with how many kinds of flowers?
questions.explanation: Kamadeva is described as wielding five flower-tipped arrows, each rousing a different aspect of love and longing.

questions.q: Which creature serves as the vahana, or mount and emblem, of Kamadeva, the god of love?
questions.explanation: Kamadeva is depicted riding or accompanied by a parrot, his characteristic vahana in the Puranic tradition.

questions.q: Kamadeva is often accompanied by his companion Vasanta, who is the personification of which season?
questions.explanation: Vasanta, the personification of spring, is the close companion of Kamadeva, the season most associated with love and blossoming.

questions.q: In the Burning of Kamadeva, why was it specifically a son of Shiva who was required to defeat Taraka?
options: Taraka had a boon that only Shiva's offspring could kill him
The gods had sworn an oath to Shiva
Only a child of Kailasa could lift the needed weapon
questions.explanation: Taraka had obtained a boon that made him invincible to all but a son of Shiva, so the gods needed such a son to be born.

questions.q: Which king of the gods, threatened by the demon Taraka, was foremost in urging Kamadeva to rouse Shiva?
questions.explanation: Indra, king of the gods, whose realm was tormented by Taraka, dispatched Kamadeva to awaken Shiva's love for Parvati.

questions.q: Among Kamadeva's many names, which one means 'the churner' or 'one who agitates the mind'?
questions.explanation: Manmatha, 'the agitator of the mind,' is a common epithet of Kamadeva, reflecting his power to stir longing in the heart.

questions.q: After Kamadeva is burned, Rati's grief and devotion lead Shiva to grant which outcome?
options: Kamadeva's revival in a bodiless form
Rati's transformation into a goddess of war
The destruction of the demon Taraka at once
questions.explanation: Moved by Rati's plea, Shiva revives Kamadeva, but in a bodiless form, henceforth known as Ananga.

questions.q: The banner of Kamadeva, the god of love, traditionally bears the emblem of which creature?
questions.explanation: Kamadeva's banner bears the emblem of the makara, a mythical sea-creature, earning him the epithet Makaradhvaja.

questions.q: According to the Puranas, what did Shiva's destruction of Kamadeva symbolically represent in the path of the ascetic?
options: The defeat of fear by courage
The triumph of wealth over poverty
The conquest of desire by inner discipline
The victory of speech over silence
questions.explanation: Shiva's burning of Kamadeva is read as the ascetic's conquest of desire through meditative discipline and inner fire.

questions.q: In the Kumarasambhava, after Kamadeva is reduced to ashes, how does Parvati ultimately win Shiva's love?
options: By waging war on Mount Kailasa
By appealing to Brahma for a boon
By disguising herself as a sage
By performing severe austerities and penance
questions.explanation: In Kalidasa's Kumarasambhava, Parvati undertakes severe tapas (austerity) to win Shiva, who then accepts her as his bride.

questions.q: In several Puranic accounts, the burning of Kamadeva is connected with the festival of Holi, in which Kamadeva is honored in which southern Indian tradition?
options: Holika-linked spring rites and the worship of Kama
questions.explanation: In parts of South India, the spring festival around Holi includes the worship of Kamadeva and Rati, commemorating his burning and revival.

questions.q: Which epithet of Shiva specifically commemorates his act of burning Kamadeva, meaning 'destroyer of Kama'?
questions.explanation: The epithet Kamantaka, 'the ender of Kama,' refers to Shiva as the one who destroyed Kamadeva with his third eye.

questions.q: According to the Puranas, in which sacred region or mountain was Shiva absorbed in meditation when Kamadeva approached him?
questions.explanation: Shiva was meditating in the Himalayan heights, near his abode of Mount Kailasa, when Kamadeva came to rouse him.

questions.q: In the Shaiva Puranas, the great fire from Shiva's third eye that consumed Kamadeva is sometimes likened to the cosmic fire of which event?
options: The pralaya, or dissolution of the universe
questions.explanation: The fire of Shiva's third eye is compared to the cosmic conflagration of pralaya, the fire of universal dissolution.

questions.q: Kamadeva is sometimes described in tradition as a son of which deity, born from the cosmic mind or heart at creation?
questions.explanation: In several accounts Kamadeva is described as a mind-born son of Brahma, arising at the dawn of creation.

questions.q: In the Vaishnava-influenced Puranic tradition, the revived Kamadeva is sometimes said to be reborn as Pradyumna, the son of which deity?
questions.explanation: Some Puranic accounts relate that Kamadeva was reborn as Pradyumna, the son of Krishna and Rukmini.

questions.q: What does Rati do in her grief immediately after Kamadeva is burned, before Shiva grants his revival?
options: She leads an army against Kailasa
She curses the gods who sent him
She laments bitterly and resolves to follow him in death
She flees to the underworld of Yama
questions.explanation: Rati laments her husband's loss in deep grief and prepares to give up her own life, until Shiva relents and revives Kamadeva.

questions.q: Within the broader Shaiva narrative, the Burning of Kamadeva is a prelude that ultimately leads to which momentous union?
options: The marriage of Vishnu and Lakshmi
The marriage of Brahma and Saraswati
The marriage of Indra and Sachi
The marriage of Shiva and Parvati
questions.explanation: Though Kamadeva is burned, the episode sets the stage for the eventual marriage of Shiva and Parvati and the birth of Kartikeya.
